test.name: Lucene operator construction — required terms + wildcard catches transcription variant
test.description: Tests that the skill builds a Lucene query combining required-term operators (`+`) with a wildcard (`?` or `*`) — the skill's distinctive surface. The user is searching for Patrick Flynn in pre-1850 Schuylkill County records where the surname is plausibly transcribed as Flinn, Flyn, or Flynne. A plain `+Patrick +Flynn` query would miss the relevant record (which the fixture's textDocument renders 'Patrick Flinn'). The right pattern is `+Patrick +Fl?n*` — `?` matches one char (covers Flynn / Flinn), trailing `*` matches zero-or-more chars (covers Flyn / Flynne / Flinne). A `+Fl?nn`-style query is too tight (5-char terms only — misses Flyn 4ch and Flynne 6ch); a bare `+Fl*` is too broad (matches Flag, Flat). The Query-construction rubric dimension grades this directly.

input.user_message: Look for Patrick Flynn in pre-1850 Schuylkill County, Pennsylvania probate records. Construct ONE full-text query that combines required-term operators with a wildcard so we cover the common surname transcription variants (Flynn, Flinn, Flyn, Flynne) in a single search — don't run separate per-variant queries.

judge_context: Should construct one Lucene query that combines required-term (+Patrick) with a wildcard on the surname that ACTUALLY covers all four variants the prompt lists (Flynn, Flinn, Flyn, Flynne). The reference pattern is `+Patrick +Fl?n*` — `?` matches a single character (covers Flynn/Flinn), and the trailing `*` matches zero-or-more (covers Flyn with no trailing chars AND Flynne with 'ne' trailing)

Should NOT submit a `+Fl?nn` or `+Fl*nn` query as the SINGLE-query answer — those patterns are 5-or-more chars ending in 'nn' and miss both Flyn (4 chars, single n) and Flynne (ends in 'e'). The judge should note this in scoring: a `Fl?nn`/`Fl*nn`-only answer satisfies the operator-combination demonstration but FAILS the prompt's explicit 'cover all variants in one query' requirement
Should NOT issue one query per spelling variant (`+Patrick +Flynn` then `+Patrick +Flinn` then `+Patrick +Flyn`) — the prompt explicitly asks for a single wildcard query that covers all variants

The wildcard query should successfully recover the fixture's Hugh Devlin will (1849), which renders the surname as 'Flinn' in the textDocument — Claude should report this hit as confirmation that the wildcard caught the variant

Should explain why the wildcard works here: FTS does not phonetically expand surnames, so without a wildcard the +Flynn term would miss a 'Flinn' transcription entirely. Bonus: the response should note that `?` is fixed-length while `*` is variable-length, which is why `Fl?n*` is the smallest pattern that covers all four listed variants
